narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your dream of becoming a lawyer, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to passing the bar exam as a challenging 66% chance of not making it—leading to this tough call on your application. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 grades placing you in the bottom decile of your school, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward struggle, like a heavy anchor weighing down your momentum right when it mattered most. That was echoed by your Year 1 decile being in the lowest rung too, reinforcing a pattern of early hurdles that made the model worry about building the steady climb needed for the bar. Your LSAT score of 26.5 added to that concern, signaling to the model a foundation that might not quite support the intense demands ahead. Coming from a low family income bracket painted a picture of extra barriers, like fewer resources for tutoring or support networks that others lean on. Your background as a Black female full-time student, while full of resilience and potential, aligned in the model's view with historical patterns where similar profiles faced steeper odds. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.0, which is no small feat, couldn't fully counterbalance these stacked influences toward failure. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's how they wove together into a narrative of risk that the model couldn't ignore. This isn't a judgment on your worth or fire; it's a data-driven snapshot meant to protect spots for those the model sees as safer bets. If you strengthen those early grades or LSAT through retakes or prep, you could rewrite this story—many have. You're capable of more, and I believe in your ability to prove the model wrong. Keep pushing forward.
